era_context: Continuation of the late Middle Bronze Age. Hammurabi of Babylon was in the early-to-middle years of his reign (traditionally dated 1792–1750 BCE, though this dating is debated). The Old Babylonian period was at its height in southern Mesopotamia. In Egypt, the 13th Dynasty continued its decline. The Hittites were consolidating power in Anatolia. Mycenaean Greece was developing palace centres.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (early Shang chronology remains uncertain)

events.title: Hammurabi's reign and Babylonian expansion
events.description: Hammurabi of Babylon was in the early-to-middle phase of his reign, during which he expanded Babylonian power through military campaigns and diplomacy. He was consolidating control over southern Mesopotamia and beginning the campaigns that would eventually make Babylon the dominant power in the region. This period saw the composition or codification of legal traditions that would culminate in the Code of Hammurabi.

sources.name: Babylonian King List (later compilation based on archival records)
Archaeological evidence from Babylon and surrounding sites

events.certainty_note: Hammurabi's reign is traditionally dated 1792–1750 BCE, making -1791 the first or second year of his rule. However, this dating is debated; some scholars place him c. 1728–1686 BCE (low chronology). The year -1792 is the conventional starting point in many chronologies, but ±20 years is realistic.

events.title: Code of Hammurabi composition and legal development
events.description: During Hammurabi's reign, legal traditions and codes were being formalized. The Code of Hammurabi, one of the earliest written legal codes, was composed during this period (traditionally dated to the later years of his reign, c. 1760 BCE, but possibly earlier). The code represented a systematization of existing legal practices and reflected Babylonian administrative sophistication. It covered property, commerce, family law, and criminal justice.

sources.name: The Code of Hammurabi (cuneiform inscriptions on stone stele and clay tablets)
Babylonian legal documents and contracts (cuneiform tablets)

events.certainty_note: The Code of Hammurabi is traditionally dated to the later years of Hammurabi's reign (c. 1760 BCE), not -1791. However, legal codification was an ongoing process throughout his reign. The year -1791 is assigned here as part of the broader legal development, but the famous stele inscription likely dates to c. 1760 BCE (±10 years).

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Hammurabi's reign is one of the most contentious issues in ancient Near Eastern chronology. The traditional 'middle chronology' places him 1792–1750 BCE, but the 'low chronology' places him c. 1728–1686 BCE, and the 'high chronology' places him c. 1848–1806 BCE. This represents a potential 120-year discrepancy. The Code of Hammurabi is often presented as a unified legal document, but it is actually a compilation of existing legal traditions, some predating Hammurabi. Its significance as a 'first written law code' is overstated; earlier Sumerian and Akkadian legal codes existed (e.g., the Code of Ur-Nammu, c. 2100 BCE).
historiographic_note: The historical record for Hammurabi's reign is relatively rich compared to contemporary Egypt, consisting of cuneiform tablets, inscriptions, and the Code itself. However, Babylonian chronology is notoriously uncertain due to competing chronological systems and the lack of absolute dating anchors (e.g., astronomical observations). The surviving record is heavily biased toward Babylon and southern Mesopotamia; northern Mesopotamia, the Levant, and other regions are poorly documented for this specific year. The Code of Hammurabi has been heavily mythologized in modern scholarship as a foundational legal document; its actual impact on contemporary legal practice is unclear.

graph_edges.note: Hammurabi's early expansion led to his later military campaigns and the peak of Babylonian power
Legal development during Hammurabi's reign culminated in the composition of the Code of Hammurabi
